meta.purpose: Reasoning chains for why a motion decision is right or wrong.
meta.load_condition: Load when the user asks why, tradeoffs, diagnosis, quality review, or strategic choice.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: A structured brief before code prevents the most expensive form of iteration: rewriting a complete animation.
logic: Writing animation code is fast for an agent; reviewing and directing it is slow for a human.
Without a brief, the human must review the entire implementation to discover the direction was wrong.
A brief costs 30 seconds of reading and can prevent 10 minutes of wasted code review.
Assumptions stated in the brief are cheap to correct; assumptions discovered in the output are expensive.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Every animation generation must start with: 'Here is my brief: [audience, attention path, tone, assumptions, success criteria]. Confirm or adjust, then I'll generate.'

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Reviewing a 3-beat minimal animation is more efficient than reviewing a 12-beat complete scene.
logic: Humans have limited review attention per cycle. After 3-4 beats, review quality degrades.
A 12-beat un-reviewed scene accumulates 12 potential judgment errors that compound.
Fixing direction on beat 3 is fast. Fixing direction after building 12 interdependent beats is a near-rewrite.
Small review cycles create a tighter feedback loop and better final quality.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Ship the smallest version that communicates the intent. Get approval. Then expand. Repeat.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Qualitative feedback ('too busy,' 'not premium enough,' 'feels off') must be translated into specific structural diagnoses before any code changes are made.
logic: Taste words are high-bandwidth human communication compressed into low-bandwidth labels.
'Too busy' = likely: too many simultaneous motion groups, insufficient stagger, no clear primary subject.
'Not premium enough' = likely: too many moving parts, too fast, wrong easing character, insufficient holds.
'Feels off' = likely: easing mismatch with brand tone, attention path contradicts information hierarchy, or timing uniformity.
Agents that act directly on taste words without diagnosis reliably apply the wrong fix — usually adjusting speed when the problem is density.
reasoning_chains.so_what: When receiving qualitative feedback, pause and respond with a diagnosis before acting: 'I interpret [taste word] as a [structural problem]. The fix would be [specific change]. Does that match what you're seeing?' Only code after confirmation.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Explicit assumption logging is not busywork. It is the mechanism that prevents agents from being confidently wrong about context they were never given.
logic: Every animation prompt is underspecified. The user says 'add animation to the dashboard' but doesn't specify device tier, brand tone, information density, user reading speed, or accessibility requirements.
Without explicit assumptions, the agent fills these gaps silently — and usually wrong. The output is technically correct for the agent's made-up context but wrong for the user's real context.
Stating assumptions makes them visible and contestable. 'I assumed mid-range mobile. Is that right?' lets the user correct the assumption before code is written.
Over multiple interactions, the assumption log builds a user profile that improves future outputs without requiring the user to restate context.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Include an assumption log in every brief. Format: 'Assumptions: I am assuming [X]. If incorrect, [Y] will need to change.' Limit to 3-5 critical assumptions per task. Focus on assumptions that, if wrong, would require major rework.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Human taste is not an obstacle to automation — it is the calibration signal that makes automation consistently good. Agents that treat human review as optional produce consistently mediocre output.
logic: Automated checks (performance, accessibility, syntax) can verify correctness. They cannot verify taste.
Taste is not magic — it is pattern recognition trained on thousands of examples. Humans have it. Agents are acquiring it.
Each review cycle where a human says 'this easing feels wrong for our brand' is a calibration signal that teaches the agent the brand's motion grammar.
After 5-10 calibration cycles on the same brand, the agent internalizes the taste patterns and human review shifts from 'direct every decision' to 'approve the direction.'
reasoning_chains.so_what: Design the workflow around review loops, not generation speed. The metric is not 'how fast can the agent generate animation' — it is 'how many review cycles to human approval.' Optimize for fewer cycles, not faster generation. Track this metric per brand and watch it decrease over time as calibration accumulates.
